_readme: Cross-language conformance fixture for tunnel bind-address validation.
WHY THIS FILE EXISTS: frontend/src/lib/ipAddress.ts hand-mirrors Go's net.ParseIP
+ IsLoopback so the Add-Tunnel dialog can tell the user WHY a bind address is
refused, instead of letting the create fail at the sidecar with a raw parse error.
The sidecar's validateTunnelBindAddr (desktop/go/tunnel.go) is the real enforcement
point either way, so drift is a bad error message rather than a security hole -- but
it is still a mirror, and a mirror nobody checks silently stops matching. This file
is that check: both suites read it, so EDITING ONE SIDE WITHOUT THE OTHER IS THE
EXACT BUG IT PREVENTS -- the language that drifts is the one whose CI turns red.
CONSUMED BY (keep both in sync with this schema):
Go: desktop/go/tunnel_test.go        TestValidateTunnelBindAddr
TS: frontend/src/lib/ipAddress.test.ts
THE CONTRACT, per case: the user typed `input` into the Add-Tunnel dialog. The
dialog normalizes it to `normalized` (that is the value it VALIDATES and the value
it SUBMITS -- see AddTunnelDialog.tsx), predicts acceptance as `loopback`, and the
sidecar then accepts `normalized` iff `loopback`. The two sides assert their own
half of that sentence:
Go asserts  validateTunnelBindAddr(normalized) == nil  <=>  loopback
TS asserts  normalizeBindAddr(input) === normalized
and isLoopbackAddress(normalized) === loopback
`normalized` is captured rather than assumed because the dialog submits it: a drift
in the bracket-stripping is a real behavioural mismatch, not just a message. Note
the sidecar never sees `input` -- only `normalized` -- which is why `[[::1]]` is
the case that pins down net.ParseIP's rejection of a bracketed literal.
`loopback` is ground truth taken from running Go, not from reading either parser.
`why` is documentation only; neither suite asserts on it.

cases.why: the canonical loopback
the whole 127.0.0.0/8 block is loopback, not just .1
bottom of the 127/8 block
top of the 127/8 block
all interfaces: the LAN-exposure case this validation exists for
private, but off-machine
private, but off-machine
just above the 127/8 block
just below the 127/8 block
octet out of range: net.ParseIP returns nil
leading zero: net.ParseIP returns nil. Number('01') === 1 waved this through once
octal-looking: net.ParseIP returns nil
leading zeros: net.ParseIP returns nil
short form: net.ParseIP returns nil
integer form: net.ParseIP returns nil
too many octets
the IPv6 loopback
uncompressed ::1 -- rejecting it here would refuse a bind the sidecar allows
fully zero-padded ::1
'::' compressing six groups
'::' compressing six groups, from the other side
IPv4-mapped loopback: To4() yields 127.0.0.1, so Go calls it loopback
IPv4-mapped, anywhere in 127/8
the same mapped address spelled in hex groups
all interfaces, v6: the LAN-exposure case
well-formed, but not loopback
one bit off ::1, and not loopback
IPv4-mapped, but the embedded v4 is not loopback
IPv4-mapped all-interfaces
IPv4-COMPATIBLE, not v4-mapped: To4() is nil and it != ::1, so Go says not loopback
link-local, not loopback
a trailing colon is only legal as part of '::'
a leading colon is only legal as part of '::'
a trailing colon is only legal as part of '::'
two '::' runs are ambiguous
three colons are not a '::' run
'::' must compress at least ONE group; this is already full length
'::' compressing nothing, trailing edition
'::' compressing nothing -- found by differentially fuzzing the TS parser against Go
nine groups
seven groups and no '::' to fill the eighth
a group is at most four hex digits
net.ParseIP does not accept a zone id (that is ParseAddr/ResolveIPAddr's job)
leading zero in the embedded v4: net.ParseIP returns nil
empty is not an address
a hostname, not an IP: net.ParseIP does not resolve
a hostname, not an IP
garbage
a hostname with dots is still not a dotted-quad
a CIDR block, not an address
a CIDR block, not an address
host:port, not an address
brackets as typed in a URL: the dialog strips them, so the sidecar sees ::1 and accepts
bracketed mapped loopback

exactly ONE bracket pair is stripped, so the sidecar sees '[::1]' -- and net.ParseIP('[::1]') is nil. Validating the stripped form while submitting the raw one is what let [::1] pass the dialog and then fail at the sidecar
surrounding whitespace is trimmed before both validation and submission
trim then unbracket
half-bracketed: nothing is stripped, and the sidecar rejects it
half-bracketed, other side
whitespace-only normalizes to empty, which is not an address
